title: Network-Mediated Capacitive Coupling Drives Fast OTOC Saturation in Superconducting Circuits
authors: Carla Caro Villanova, Alan C. Santos
abstract: We investigate the dynamical and spectral consequences of capacitance-network-mediated interactions in superconducting transmon arrays beyond effective nearest-neighbor descriptions. While weak coupling regimes are well captured by an effective nearest-neighbor interacting models, we show that increasing capacitive connectivity induces a pronounced departure from this approximation in dynamical observables. Using Out-of-Time-Ordered Correlators (OTOCs), we demonstrate that such network-mediated couplings significantly accelerate operator scrambling, leading to rapid saturation compared to the nearest-neighbor limit. This dynamical crossover is accompanied by a shift in spectral statistics away from Poissonian behavior toward level repulsion, with the ratio parameter remaining intermediate between Poisson and Gaussian Orthogonal Ensemble (GOE) limits. This indicates the emergence of partial ergodicity rather than fully developed quantum chaos. As this behavior arises within experimentally realistic regimes of current superconducting transmon devices, identifying when network-mediated couplings qualitatively alter information dynamics is directly relevant for scalable superconducting architectures.
summary: The study shows that the capacitance network in transmon arrays induces non-local interactions that significantly speed up the scrambling of quantum information. While these interactions degrade single-qubit state transfer, they facilitate faster OTOC saturation and move the system toward partial ergodicity.
main_problem: Investigating how non-local, network-mediated capacitive interactions in superconducting transmon arrays affect quantum information scrambling and spectral statistics.
main_result: Non-local couplings accelerate the saturation of Out-of_Time-Ordered Correlators (OTOCs) and drive the system toward partial ergodicity, though they do not reach full quantum chaos (GOE).
method: Analytical derivation using Chebyshev polynomials of the second kind to invert the capacitance matrix, combined with exact numerical diagonalization and OTOC calculations.
model_or_system: A 1D array of N superconducting transmon qubits with interactions derived from the inverse capacitance matrix, including nearest-neighbor and non-local 'collateral' couplings.
key_observables: Out-of-Time-Ordered Correlators (OTOCs), level-spacing ratio (r-parameter), and single-excitation state transfer efficiency.
important_parameters: Capacitance ratio (Cc/Cq), Manhattan distance between qubits, and the weak-coupling limit.
main_assumptions: Two-level system (TLS) approximation, Rotating Wave Approximation (RWA), and the transmon approximation (expanding cosine potential to fourth order).
figures_summary: Figures 4a and 4b show OTOC dynamics for 10 and 12 qubit chains, illustrating early-time N.N. dominance and late-time non-local effects; Figure 4c shows the transition of the level-spacing ratio with increasing qubit number.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of non-local capacitive coupling, provides an analytical framework for the capacitance network, analyzes the impact on single-excitation transport, investigates many-body scrambling via OTOCs, and concludes with spectral statistics analysis.
why_interesting: It provides insights into how parasitic or network-induced non-localities can be leveraged to accelerate information scrambling and create non-local correlations without entering a fully chaotic regime, which is crucial for scalable superconducting architectures.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. (a) Proposal of a device enabling the experimental implementation of our results, in which a linear chain of transmon qubits can be locally controlled and multi-qubit operations can be done through nearest-neighbor capacitive interactions. (b) Sketch of the device showing the geometrical meaning of the Euclidean (real) and Manhattan distances. Direct parasitic capacitance scales with the Euclidean distance [cite]. Conversely, the collateral coupling derived in this work does not follow the Euclidean distance, but rather propagates through the discrete topological links (coupling capacitors), scaling with the “Manhattan distance”, dmn = |⃗n −⃗m|, across the network graph.
